attributes.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of fever, sore throat, and a rapidly spreading rash. She started taking allopurinol for gout five days ago. She has no other significant medical history and takes no other regular medications. On examination, she appears unwell with a pulse rate of 110/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. Painful erosions are noted on her oral mucosa, with several flaccid bullae and widespread erythema with epidermal detachment over 40% of her body as shown (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is presenting with toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), a severe cutaneous adverse reaction most commonly triggered by medications. As the skin detachment is over 30% of her body, it is classified as TEN. If it was less than 10% skin detachment of her body surface area, this is clinically diagnosed as Steven-Johnson's Syndrome (SJS). The most appropriate next step in management is intravenous hydration. TEN is a medical emergency with a high mortality rate, and early transfer to a specialized burn unit may be associated with improved outcomes to prevent sepsis and multiorgan failure. Intravenous or nasogastric fluid replacement, electrolyte management, analgesia and careful skin care with dressings as appropriate are essential supportive management.

Administration of intravenous corticosteroids is controversial in TEN. While some studies have shown benefit, others suggest they may increase the risk of sepsis and mortality. Its use is not universally recommended and should not delay transfer to a specialized unit or compensating for loss of hydration. Similarly, IV immunoglobulins, cyclosporine and anti-TNFα monoclonal antibodies may have some benefit in managing TEN but further studies are required.

IV Benzylpenicillin is not indicated in the management of TEN unless there is a confirmed secondary bacterial infection. The primary focus should be on supportive care.

Continuation of allopurinol would be inappropriate. Immediate discontinuation of the suspected causative drug is crucial in managing TEN. In this case, allopurinol is likely the trigger and should be stopped immediately. Carbamazepine, lamotrigine, sulfonamide antibiotics and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are other common triggers for TEN and SJS. Human leukocyte antigen (HLA) types have been strongly linked with increased risk of SJS and TEN, especially in patients of Asian decent. Testing for HLA types prior to commencing certain medications may be considered to prevent TEN and SJS.

Reduce room temperature to cool 28.0°C is not correct in the initial management of TEN. Setting an ambient room temperature of 31°C–32°C in a specialised burns unit is part of supportive care for SJS and TEN.
learningPoints: Immediate discontinuation of the offending drug is crucial in the management of toxic epidermal necrolysis.
Supportive care, including fluid and electrolyte management, is essential in treating toxic epidermal necrolysis.
references: Ngan V, Oakley A, Dyall-Smitt D. Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis. In: DermnetNZ. [online]. 2016. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/stevens-johnson-syndrome-toxic-epidermal-necrolysis

Torres-Navarro I, Briz-Redon A, Botella-Estrada R. Systemic therapies for Stevens-Johnson Syndrome and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: a SCORTEN-based systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology. 2021: 35 (1). Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32946187/

Kumar R, Das A, Das S. Management of Stevens-Johnson Syndrome -Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: Looking Beyond Guidelines! Indian Journal of Dermatology. 2018: 63 (2). Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5903040/
